preFix.title: Home composting vs council food waste collection
preFix.subtitle: When to compost at home and when the brown bin is the better choice
preFix.excerpt: Home composting and council food waste collection both divert organic waste from landfill, but they are not equivalent. Home composting returns nutrients to your own garden; the council collection goes to industrial anaerobic digestion or composting. This is the decision framework for which to use, when to use both, and which materials each handles best.
preFix.sourceNotes: WRAP UK Composting and Anaerobic Digestion evidence review (2023). DEFRA waste hierarchy guidance. Garden Organic home composting factsheets. UK government 'Consistent collections' policy (all English councils to provide weekly food waste collection by 2026).

content.text: Both home composting and
food waste collection
divert organic waste from landfill or incineration, but they serve different purposes. Home composting returns nutrients to your soil directly.
Anaerobic digestion
handles materials that cannot safely go in a home bin. Using both is the best approach for most households.
What goes in the home compost bin
All fruit and vegetable peelings and off-cuts (raw)
Teabags (loose or compostable only; check for polypropylene mesh which does not compost)
Coffee grounds
Garden waste (grass clippings, autumn leaves, soft prunings)
Cardboard (torn and wetted), plain paper, egg boxes
What goes in the council food waste bin
Cooked food waste (pasta, rice, bread, cooked vegetables)
Meat and fish (raw or cooked)
Dairy products
Bones, shells, eggshells (where your council accepts them; check locally)
Why these materials differ

Cooked food, meat, and dairy attract vermin, encourage flies, and introduce pathogens that most domestic compost heaps do not reach temperatures high enough to destroy. Industrial composting and AD processes at 55 to 70°C kill these pathogens reliably. They also produce energy as biogas from the AD process, making them more valuable in the industrial stream than in a home bin.
When home composting is clearly better

Home composting produces finished compost that you use on your own garden. If you have a garden and a composting space, composting your raw vegetable waste at home closes the nutrient loop: the soil feeds the garden, the garden feeds the kitchen, the kitchen feeds the heap, and the heap feeds the soil. The council bin takes the waste away; you never see the digestate or compost it produces. For the home gardener, home composting is better than collection for raw vegetable waste.
When council collection is better

If you have no garden or outdoor space, the council collection is your only option. If you generate cooked food waste, meat, or dairy, the council collection handles these safely; home composting cannot. Using both is the best approach: council bin for cooked waste and meat, home bin for raw vegetable waste.
